verdict_reasoning: HSPA1A (HSP70/HSP72) is canonically a cytosolic chaperone with no transmembrane domain, GPI anchor, or classical surface-targeting signal. However, extensive published evidence documents tumor-cell-specific plasma membrane display: (1) screening of ~1,000 primary human tumor biopsies showed carcinomas, but not normal tissues, present HSP70 on their surface; (2) flow cytometry on intact viable tumor cells with the cmHsp70.1 mAb — which recognises the extracellular-exposed C-terminal TKD epitope — confirms outer-leaflet accessibility in multiple tumor lines; (3) surface iodination and global plasma-membrane proteomics independently detect HSPA1A on tumor cell surfaces; (4) the membrane anchor appears to be interaction with tumor-specific sphingoglycolipids/phosphatidylserine in cholesterol-rich microdomains. This surface display is absent on normal (non-transformed) tissues and is a tumor-state (oncogenic transformation/stress) phenomenon. Additionally, stress-induced (heat, infection, hypoxia) upregulation and active secretion contribute to extracellular pools. The surface form is thus cell_state_induced (oncogenic transformation), qualifying as contextual. Pre-no checklist: not lysosomal-TM cycling; no germline/tissue-restricted anchor; the PM pool is documented by direct flow/biotinylation evidence specifically for HSPA1A in tumor/stress state, ruling out secreted_only.
